markdown:
1. The GPT-3 paper attributes one specific component to GPT-2: it reuses the byte-level BPE tokenizer of GPT-2, noting that this tokenizer was developed for an almost entirely English training dataset. [1]
2. Within the supplied evidence passages, no passage from the GPT-3 paper cites GPT-2 as an architectural predecessor; the only GPT-2 attribution appearing in the supplied GPT-3 evidence concerns tokenizer reuse. [1][2][3][4]
3. The GPT-2 paper states in its Model section that it uses a Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) based architecture for its language models, and that the model largely follows the details of the OpenAI GPT model (Radford et al., 2018) with a few modifications. [5]
4. The GPT-2 paper also names the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) in its Approach section as an example of a self-attention architecture that has improved the expressiveness of models computing conditional token probabilities. [6]
5. No claim is made of a direct GPT-3 to Attention Is All You Need citation; the lineage is constructed only as GPT-3 → GPT-2 (tokenizer reuse) and GPT-2 → Attention Is All You Need (Transformer architecture attribution). [1][5][6]

Sources
[1] Language Models are Few-Shot Learners, 3.3 Translation, p. 14
[2] Language Models are Few-Shot Learners, 3.3 Translation, p. 15
[3] Language Models are Few-Shot Learners, 3.9.4 News Article Generation, p. 25
[4] Language Models are Few-Shot Learners, 3.9.4 News Article Generation, p. 26
[5] Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners, 2.3. Model, p. 4
[6] Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners, 2. Approach, p. 2
summary: The GPT-3 paper attributes one specific component to GPT-2: it reuses the byte-level BPE tokenizer of GPT-2, noting that this tokenizer was developed for an almost entirely English training dataset.
